purpose: Uses the 1990s telecom infrastructure boom as a capital cycle analogue to assess likely investor outcomes in the energy transition build-out.
components: Construction phase: massive capital deployment, many entrants, aggressive valuations
Bust phase: 95% company failure, median investor IRR slightly negative
Legacy phase: surviving infrastructure enables later revolution for different beneficiaries
IRA modification: subsidy floor as structural difference potentially improving investor outcomes vs telecom
Key insight: value accrues to different entities than those who funded construction
current_assessment: The structural parallel is strong. The IRA subsidy floor is a genuine difference but its impact on the IRR distribution is unquantified. Grid infrastructure thesis for electrical equipment manufacturers (Eaton, Hubbell, Schneider) with visible order books may represent the 'infrastructure layer' that survives.
limitations: Neither the analogue nor any debater quantified how much IRA shifts investor outcomes
Telecom was unregulated; energy has regulated utility returns providing a floor
Timing of bust phase is unspecified
